title-group.article-title: Postoperative nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drug prophylaxis for elbow heterotopic ossification: a systematic review and meta-analysis comparing COX-2 selective and nonselective inhibitors

contrib-group.aff: aRothman Orthopaedic Institute, AdventHealth, Orlando, FL, USA
bUniversity of Central Florida College of Medicine, Orlando, FL, USA
cRothman Florida Elbow Experts (ROFLEX) Group, Orlando, FL, USA

author-notes.corresp: ∗Corresponding author: Luke S. Oh, MD, MS, Sports Medicine Orthopaedic Surgeon Rothman Orthopaedic Institute, Advent Health, 265 East Rollins Street, 11th floor, Orlando, FL 32751, USA. luke.oh@rothmanortho.com

license.license-p: This is an open access article under the CC BY-NC-ND license (http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc-nd/4.0/).

sec.title: Background
sec.p: Heterotopic ossification (HO) is a significant complication following elbow trauma and surgery, leading to pain, stiffness, and functional impairment. While nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drugs (NSAIDs) have been extensively investigated for HO prophylaxis, their effectiveness in preventing postoperative HO in the elbow remains unclear. This study aims to compare the efficacy of selective vs. nonselective NSAIDs in reducing postoperative HO rates after traumatic elbow surgeries.

sec.title: Methods
sec.p: This systematic review and meta-analysis were conducted following Preferred Reporting Items for Systematic Reviews and Meta-Analyses guidelines. A comprehensive search was performed in PubMed, Embase, Cochrane Library, and Web of Science from January 2004 to January 10, 2025. Level I-III studies were included if they examined patients who underwent elbow surgery following trauma and compared selective COX-2 inhibitors or nonselective NSAIDs to no prophylaxis, with reported postoperative HO formation rates.

sec.title: Results
sec.p: A total of 2,429 articles were identified across the four databases. Following full-text review, 1 randomized control trial and 5 retrospective studies were included in the quantitative synthesis, comprising patients who underwent either acute post-traumatic surgery or postexcision/open arthrolysis for established HO. Both selective (celecoxib) and nonselective (indomethacin) NSAIDs demonstrated no statistically significant difference in reducing postoperative HO compared with controls (celecoxib: risk ratio = 0.64, 95% confidence interval 0.32-1.31, P = .22; indomethacin: risk ratio = 0.87, 95% confidence interval 0.65-1.18, P = .38). Nonselective (indomethacin and ibuprofen) and selective (celecoxib) NSAID prophylaxis significantly reduced HO incidence compared to controls (P = .007), demonstrating a 27% relative risk reduction.

sec.title: Conclusion
sec.p: This study demonstrates that both selective (celecoxib) and nonselective (indomethacin and ibuprofen) NSAIDs effectively reduce the risk of HO following elbow trauma surgery. When analyzed individually, neither the selective COX-2 inhibitor (celecoxib) nor the nonselective NSAIDs (indomethacin, ibuprofen) showed a statistically significant difference compared with controls, indicating no clear difference in efficacy between NSAID classes. However, given the limited number of studies and interstudy heterogeneity, the overall power of the current evidence is low, and further prospective research is needed to validate these findings.

body.p: Heterotopic ossification (HO) is a pathological condition characterized by the formation of bone in any extraosseous site, including but not limited to tendons, ligaments, skeletal muscle, fascia, and virtually any area of connective tissue.23 HO can be acquired through rare genetic mutations17 or from complications after trauma, burns, neurological injury, and surgical interventions.4,8,10,35 Nongenetic HO can present in any body area but it most commonly develops in areas susceptible to trauma such as the hip, knee, shoulder, and elbow.21 Among these, the elbow is particularly vulnerable to HO after trauma and surgery, with the formation being reported in up to 89% of cases,25 often leading to pain, stiffness, and significant functional impairments that make routine activities difficult for affected individuals.

While several treatment modalities for HO have been well established, nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drugs (NSAIDs) have become a leading choice of pharmacological prophylaxis.26 NSAID prophylaxis in preventing the recurrence of HO in the hip has been well explored,30,37 with numerous studies comparing the effectiveness of selective COX-2 and nonselective COX inhibition.11,36,40 Yet the efficacy of these agents in preventing HO in the elbow remains unclear.2,33

This systematic review and meta-analysis aims to evaluate the efficacy of selective and nonselective NSAID prophylaxis for preventing postoperative HO recurrence in the elbow following trauma. It will analyze studies comparing patients who received selective and nonselective NSAID prophylaxis after surgery following elbow trauma to those who did not, focusing on reported HO recurrence rates. By synthesizing the available evidence, this review seeks to identify critical gaps in the literature and propose directions for future research to optimize NSAID prophylactic regimens for elbow HO prevention.

sec.title: Registration and protocol
sec.p: This systematic review adhered to the Preferred Reporting Items for Systematic Reviews and Meta-Analyses guidelines.20 The review was registered in the International Prospective Register of Systematic Reviews (ID: CRD42025635952).

sec.title: Search strategy
sec.p: Two researchers (AA and RK) systematically searched PubMed, Embase, Cochrane Library, and Web of Science from January 2004 until January 10th, 2025, using Medical Subject Headings and a combination of keywords and controlled vocabulary for the concepts shown in Supplementary Figure S1. Studies published more than 20 years ago were excluded to maintain relevance with contemporary surgical techniques, rehabilitation protocols, imaging standards, and currently used NSAID formulations/dosing.

sec.title: Eligibility criteria
sec.p: Two authors (AA and RK) independently screened titles and abstracts. Duplicates, non-English papers, case reports, and biomechanical/cadaveric studies were excluded. The full texts of the potentially eligible articles were then screened considering the inclusion and exclusion criteria. The inclusion criteria were: (1) human studies that compared selective COX-2 inhibitors or nonselective NSAIDs with no prophylaxis and reported HO formation rate after elbow trauma or surgery; and (2) studies reporting rates of radiographic HO recurrence or progression at various postoperative time points. Exclusion criteria included: (1) studies without control groups, as this review aims to analyze the effect of selective or nonselective NSAID prophylaxis compared to no prophylaxis at all; (2) HO in joints other than the elbow, unless elbow-specific data could be separated and analyzed; (3) HO resulting from neurological, burn, or spinal cord injuries; (4) nonpharmacologic treatments, including physical therapy, radiation, or surgical interventions without selective or nonselective NSAID prophylaxis; (5) animal studies, cadaveric specimens, and in vitro experiments; (6) case reports, narrative reviews, expert opinions, conference abstracts, and editorial articles; and (7) studies published more than 20 years ago and non-English publications without accessible translations.

sec.title: Selection process
sec.p: Two authors (AA and RK) independently screened the titles and abstracts of articles that met the preliminary inclusion criteria. In addition, we reviewed the reference list of a relevant systematic review comparing prophylaxis options for elbow HO15 to identify further eligible studies. The full-text screening process was conducted afterward, and any discrepancies during this phase were resolved through discussion with the senior author (ARK).

sec.title: Data extraction
sec.p:
Two reviewers (AA and RK) separately collected data from the full texts of the included studies using a predesigned Excel sheet. The extracted data were compared and cross-checked, with discrepancies resolved through consensus and discussion with the senior author (ARK). The comprehensive extracted data is reported in Tables I and II.Table I
Study characteristics.

Haglin, 2018128345.219-8158NSAID vs. none6 weeksPostop
Taralloa, 201734315224-8068NSAID vs. none200 mg 2X a d2 weeksPostop
Rashid, 2015274244NSAID vs. none25 mg 3X a d21 dPostop
Sun, 20153315243.918-8249NSAID vs. none200 mg 1X a d28 dPostop
NSAIDs, nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drugs; Postop, postoperative.Table II
Included studies reports on HO rate.

HO, heterotopic ossification; RCT, randomized controlled trial; F/U, follow-up; NSAIDs, nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drugs; Preop, preoperative; Postop, postoperative.

sec.title: Statistical analysis
sec.p: We performed a meta-analysis using risk ratios (RRs) with 95% confidence intervals (CIs) to compare the rates of HO incidence or recurrence in patients receiving selective COX-2 inhibitors or nonselective NSAIDs vs. those not receiving prophylaxis. A P value of less than .05 was considered statistically significant.

We chose RR as the effect measure because it is the most appropriate for comparing the likelihood of a binary outcome between two groups. The choice of a random-effects model was justified by the expected heterogeneity between studies, given differences in patient populations, treatment regimen, and follow-up time period. Heterogeneity was assessed using the I2 statistic, with values greater than 50% indicating significant heterogeneity. A fixed-effects model was used when heterogeneity was low (I2 ≤ 50%), whereas a random-effects model was applied when heterogeneity was high (I2 > 50%).

Intervention arms with fewer than 5 patients were excluded from the analysis to avoid bias introduced by small sample sizes. Subgroup analyses were conducted to evaluate the effects of specific NSAIDs vs. no prophylaxis with statistical methods consistent with the overall analysis. The statistical software used was Revman 5.4 (The Cochrane Collaboration, London, United Kingdom), and the Mantel–Haenszel method was used for both fixed- and random-effects models.

sec.title: Risk of bias
sec.p: We assessed the quality of the included studies using the Cochrane Risk of Bias assessment tool for the randomized controlled trials (RCT)s and the Newcastle–Ottawa Scale for the retrospective cohort studies.

The Cochrane Risk of Bias assessment tool evaluates five key domains: randomization process, deviations from intended interventions, missing outcome data, measurement of the outcome, and selection of the reported result.32 Studies are categorized as having a low risk of bias, some concerns, or high risk of bias for each domain, with an overall risk-of-bias rating assigned.

The Newcastle–Ottawa Scale evaluates observational study quality across three domains: (1) selection (up to 4 points), which considers how representative the exposed and nonexposed cohorts are, how exposure was determined, and whether the outcome was absent at baseline; (2) comparability (up to 2 points), which assesses how well the study accounts for potential confounding variables; and (3) outcome (up to 3 points), which evaluates outcome assessment methods, adequacy of follow-up duration, and completeness of follow-up.31 Based on these criteria, each study is awarded a maximum score of 9 points. Studies scoring ≥7 points are generally considered to have higher methodological quality, while those scoring 4-6 points are considered moderate quality, and those scoring ≤3 points indicate a higher risk of bias, and lower methodological rigor.

sec.title: Study selection
sec.p:
A total of 2,429 articles were retrieved across all four databases, 35 of which met the preliminary inclusion criteria after title and abstract screening. Twenty additional studies from reference lists of relevant systematic reviews were also retrieved. After the final stage of screening, 6 studies remained for our quantitative synthesis (Fig. 1).Figure 1
PRISMA flow diagram. ∗Consider, if feasible to do so, reporting the number of records identified from each database or register searched (rather than the total number across all databases/registers). ∗∗If automation tools were used, indicate how many records were excluded by a human and how many were excluded by automation tools. From: Page MJ, McKenzie JE, Bossuyt PM, Boutron I, Hoffmann TC, Mulrow CD, et al. The PRISMA 2020 statement: an updated guideline for reporting systematic reviews. BMJ 2021;372:n71. https://doi.org/10.1136/bmj.n71. For more information, visit: http://www.prisma-statement.org/. PRISMA, Preferred Reporting Items for Systematic Reviews and Meta-Analyses; HO, heterotopic ossification.

sec.title: Quality assessment
sec.p: The overall quality of the studies included was acceptable, with one RCT having a low risk of bias (Supplementary Table S1) and five retrospective cohort studies ranging from moderate to high quality based on their respective scales (Supplementary Table S2). While some studies had limitations in confounder adjustment and follow-up consistency, the evidence provides a reasonable basis for the analysis of selective and nonselective COX-2 inhibitors prophylaxis in elbow HO.

sec.title: Study characteristics
sec.p: Of the six final studies, one was a RCT2 and the other five were retrospective cohort studies.5,12,27,33,34 In total, 610 patients who sustained elbow trauma and later underwent surgery were included across both the intervention and control groups. Four studies investigated patients with acute elbow fractures or dislocations who underwent open reduction and internal fixation or radial head arthroplasty within a timeframe ranging from four days to three weeks postinjury, totaling 375 patients. The remaining two studies focused on patients with post-traumatic elbow stiffness and preoperatively confirmed HO who later underwent open elbow arthrolysis with HO excision at a delayed interval of 6-11 months postinjury, comprising 235 patients. Regarding NSAID prophylaxis, three studies compared indomethacin vs. no prophylaxis, while two studies compared celecoxib vs. no prophylaxis. One study included multiple NSAIDs, with patients receiving celecoxib (n = 54), ibuprofen (n = 18), indomethacin (n = 3), meloxicam (n = 1), and naproxen (n = 2) vs. no prophylaxis. However, treatment arms with sample sizes less than five were excluded from the pooled analysis to maintain statistical reliability. Across all studies, mean age and sex distribution were comparable between intervention and control groups (Table I).

sec.title: Nonselective vs. control
sec.p:
Three studies reported the use of indomethacin, with 110 patients treated with the intervention and 173 patients in the control group. Heterogeneity analysis showed good homogeneity among studies (P = .95; I2 = 0%), prompting the use of a fixed-effects model. Rates of HO were not significantly lower in the indomethacin-treated group compared to controls (RR = 0.87; CI: [0.65, 1.18]; P = .38), suggesting patients who received indomethacin did not have a significantly decreased risk of HO compared to controls (Fig. 2).Figure 2
Forest plot for rates of heterotopic ossification for patients who received indomethacin vs. controls. M–H, Mantel–Haenszel; CI, confidence interval.

sec.title: Selective vs. control
sec.p:
Three studies reported the use of celecoxib, with 144 patients treated with the intervention and 165 patients in the control group. Heterogeneity analysis showed low homogeneity among studies (P = .06; I2 = 65%), prompting the use of a random-effects model. Rates of HO were not significantly lower in the celecoxib-treated group compared to controls (RR = 0.64; CI: [0.32, 1.31]; P = .22), suggesting patients who received celecoxib did not have a significantly decreased risk of HO compared to controls (Fig. 3).Figure 3
Forest plot for rates of heterotopic ossification for patients who received celecoxib vs. controls. M–H, Mantel–Haenszel; CI, confidence interval.

sec.title: Nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drug vs. control
sec.p:
HO formation rate was the primary outcome measure in most studies, and all studies reported HO rates as RRs or percentages (Table II). Our analysis contained all six studies, consisting of 254 patients treated with indomethacin, ibuprofen, and celecoxib, and 338 patients in the control group. Heterogeneity analysis showed good homogeneity among studies (P = .09; I2 = 46%), prompting the use of a fixed-effects model. Rates of HO were significantly lower in the NSAID-treated group compared to controls (RR = 0.73; CI: [0.59, 0.92]; P = .007), indicating a 27% relative reduction in the risk of developing HO in patients who received NSAID prophylaxis compared to controls (Fig. 4).Figure 4
Forest plot for rates of heterotopic ossification for patients who received NSAIDs vs. controls. NSAID, nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drug; M–H, Mantel–Haenszel; CI, confidence interval.

sec.title: Discussion
sec.p: The findings of this systematic review and meta-analysis revealed a significant reduction in HO rates among patients who received postoperative NSAID prophylaxis following elbow trauma compared to controls. This finding is particularly relevant given recent studies showing no significant difference between NSAID prophylaxis and no prophylaxis in patients sustaining elbow trauma.2,9 In our subgroup analyses, there was no discernible difference in HO prevention between indomethacin and celecoxib. This aligns with Wörner et al39 who found no significant difference between indomethacin and meloxicam in preventing postoperative HO in patients undergoing primary distal biceps tendon repair.

Over the past few decades, NSAIDs have been recognized as effective in preventing HO following hip arthroplasty. Various NSAIDs have been suggested for prophylactic use, with indomethacin being the most extensively investigated. However, determining the most suitable drug for HO prevention remains challenging due to balancing efficacy with safety. Romano et al29 reported that while indomethacin has been used for this purpose, it is associated with adverse effects such as gastrointestinal complications, excessive bleeding, and mental confusion.

While our study found no superiority in HO prevention between selective and nonselective NSAIDs, previous studies in total hip arthroplasty have demonstrated a significant reduction in HO recurrence with celecoxib compared to ibuprofen, suggesting that selective COX-2 inhibitors may be more effective than nonselective NSAIDs.40 Given the limited number of studies examining NSAID prophylaxis for HO in the elbow, future studies should investigate whether the superior efficacy of selective COX-2 inhibitors observed in total hip arthroplasty translates to improved HO prevention in the elbow. Clarifying these relationships will help guide NSAID selection and improve prophylactic strategies for elbow HO prevention.

The inclusion of patients who underwent surgical excision of pre-existing HO presents a unique consideration in our study. Importantly, all patients in our meta-analysis initially underwent surgery for elbow trauma, but in some cases, HO had already developed prior to their operative intervention and required excision. Due to the limited number of available studies, stratified analyses separating acute trauma from postexcision cases could not be performed, which may have influenced pooled estimates. These two populations likely differ in inflammatory activity and baseline risk, and future research should examine them separately to clarify potential differences in prophylactic response. Nonetheless, surgical intervention itself can reinitiate the local inflammatory cascade that drives heterotopic bone formation, making postoperative prophylaxis biologically relevant in both settings. A study by Sun et al33 evaluated celecoxib prophylaxis after HO excision for post-traumatic elbow stiffness and demonstrated that celecoxib reduced HO recurrence at 3, 6, and 9 months postoperatively compared with controls, and that patients not receiving prophylaxis were more likely to develop severe HO. While limited to a single cohort type, these findings provide insight into this secondary-prevention setting and highlight the continued relevance of NSAID prophylaxis in established, trauma-related HO.

The role of NSAIDs in reducing the severity of HO, which can directly impact function, pain, and the need for revision surgery, is an important clinical consideration. Many radiographic classification systems have been developed to assess the extent of HO around soft tissue and its impact on functional activity. The Brooker classification, widely used in the hip, evaluates the severity of HO formation and its potential to impair joint mobility.6 In contrast, the Hastings and Graham classification has recently emerged as a grading system specifically designed for elbow HO, accounting for the elbow's unique biomechanical and anatomical complexity.14 The studies included in our meta-analysis used either the Brooker or Hastings and Graham classification, but because these systems assess HO severity differently, direct comparisons across studies remain challenging.

Several studies in hip arthroplasty have investigated the impact of prophylaxis vs. no prophylaxis on HO severity grading. Notably, celecoxib has been shown to reduce the incidence of mild HO (grade I and II) compared to control groups using the Brooker classification.3 However, emerging research found no significant correlation between HO severity and the use of prophylaxis at six weeks,18 highlighting the ongoing debate regarding the true impact of NSAID prophylaxis on HO severity. Future studies should aim to further elucidate the impact of different NSAID regimens on HO severity, ideally through larger sample sizes and standardized severity grading. The studies included in our analysis did not provide sufficient data to conduct a robust analysis on the relationship between NSAID prophylaxis and HO severity, highlighting a key gap in the current literature. In addition, the functional implications of HO, particularly its effects on postoperative range of motion, pain, and patient satisfaction, remain under-reported in existing studies and should be incorporated as key outcome measures in future research.

The timing of NSAID administration is another crucial factor influencing HO prevention. HO formation occurs in distinct phases, beginning with the inflammatory phase immediately after trauma, during which mesenchymal stem cells differentiate into osteoblasts in the surrounding soft tissue.22 This phase occurs within hours to days of the initial injury, while the ossification and maturation phase typically takes six weeks before HO becomes radiographically detectable.24 All studies included in our meta-analysis assessed radiographic evidence of HO after this maturation period, ensuring that only mature HO was analyzed and ruling out early-stage dystrophic calcifications as potential confounders. Li et al19 demonstrated that patients who received celecoxib immediately after elbow surgery had a lower risk of HO progression than those who initiated celecoxib two months later, supporting the importance of early NSAID administration.

When comparing prophylactic options for HO, many studies have found no significant difference between radiation therapy and pharmacological prophylaxis in preventing recurrence.15,27 However, conflicting evidence remains, with some studies supporting radiation therapy's effectiveness,16,28 while others cite increased adverse events and concerns about nonunion formation and impaired wound healing.13 Further research is needed to clarify the long-term safety and efficacy of NSAID prophylaxis compared to radiotherapy, but our findings suggest NSAID prophylaxis is an efficacious option.

While NSAIDs have demonstrated efficacy in reducing HO incidence, providers must also consider their potential impact on soft-tissue and osseous healing. Earlier studies suggested an association between NSAID use and increased nonunion rates, raising concerns about their routine use in orthopedic patients.1,38 However, more recent studies have challenged this notion, with some findings indicating no significant difference in fracture healing outcomes between patients who received NSAIDs at typical therapeutic dosages and those who did not.7 Given these conflicting perspectives, further research is needed to clarify the true impact of NSAID prophylaxis on bone healing, particularly in the context of HO prevention.

Despite the strengths of our meta-analysis, several limitations must be acknowledged. Variability in NSAID dosing and duration, patient comorbidities, differences in injury severity and type, and inconsistencies in surgical procedures introduce potential confounders. In addition, the small number of available studies, combined with their retrospective design and heterogeneity in study protocols, limits the statistical power and generalizability of our findings. Furthermore, while our analysis assessed HO incidence, we were unable to evaluate HO severity, which is crucial in determining its functional impact and the need for additional interventions.

Moving forward, standardized prophylactic protocols, injury-specific classification systems, and stratified analyses accounting for injury mechanism and patient variability will be essential to refining HO prevention strategies in the elbow. Future research should aim to establish optimal NSAID regimens, determine whether selective COX-2 inhibitors offer advantages in safety and/or efficacy over nonselective NSAIDs, and to evaluate the role of multimodal prophylaxis approaches. Future prospective studies should aim for greater homogeneity by adopting standardized reporting practices. Detailed documentation of additional interventions—such as radiotherapy, corticosteroid injections, or blood flow restriction—should include the dosage, number of sessions, and timing relative to surgery. Consistent use of the Hastings and Graham classification for grading elbow HO severity is encouraged to facilitate reliable comparisons across studies. In addition, standardized outcome measures, including postoperative range of motion, Mayo Elbow Performance Index, visual analog scale for pain, and functional assessments such as Disabilities of the Arm, Shoulder, and Hand or 12-Item Short Form Survey, should be incorporated. Uniform reporting of these parameters will enhance comparability and strengthen future meta-analyses. Ultimately, improving our understanding of HO prevention in the elbow will enable more effective, individualized treatment strategies to optimize patient outcomes.

sec.title: Conclusion
sec.p: This study demonstrates that both selective (celecoxib) and nonselective (indomethacin and ibuprofen) NSAIDs effectively reduce the risk of HO following elbow trauma surgery. When analyzed individually, neither the selective COX-2 inhibitor (celecoxib) nor the nonselective NSAIDs (indomethacin, ibuprofen) showed a statistically significant difference compared with controls, indicating no clear difference in efficacy between NSAID classes. However, given the limited number of studies and interstudy heterogeneity, the overall power of the current evidence is low, and further prospective research is needed to validate these findings.

sec.title: Disclaimers:
sec.p: Funding: No funding was disclosed by the authors.

Conflicts of interest: The authors, their immediate family, or any research foundation with which they are affiliated did not receive any financial payments or other benefits from any commercial entity related to the subject of this article.

fn.p: Institutional review board approval was not required for this systematic review.
Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.xrrt.2025.100628.
